What Is Encryption? Why Do We Encrypt? A Beginner's Take
What Is Encryption? Why Do We Encrypt?
What is encryption? It's a fun topic, and something came up recently that nudged me to write this — so here's a beginner-friendly take on what encryption actually is.
In short, encryption is the process of turning
Plaintext is text you can read directly. For example:
This is some text — currently in plaintext form.
Ciphertext is text you can't read directly. The same text might become:
. To decrypt the ciphertext back into plaintext, you need the matching key.

Let's frame it with an example. You've got a site with a login system. When a user logs in, they submit a username and password, the server compares the submitted password to what's stored in the database, and returns success or failure.
Now the problem — for the server to do that check, the database has to store the user's password somehow. If a hacker breaches the database, they walk away with everyone's passwords. Major problem.
So for sensitive data like passwords, we store an encrypted version. Even if a hacker grabs the data, they don't see plaintext passwords — they see scrambled bytes. Much better.
Of course, if your key is
stolen, the encryption is moot. So how you protect the key matters as much as the encryption itself.
There are two main families of encryption:
Each one uses different algorithms — AES, RSA, etc. Those algorithms are all doing the same general job: encrypting and decrypting via a key.

First, symmetric encryption — same key used to both encrypt and decrypt. If our key is "IsRayNotArray", we use that key for both directions. The same key turns plaintext into ciphertext and vice versa.

But there's a catch. If you want to send an encrypted message to someone else, you also need to give them the key — otherwise they can't decrypt. And if someone intercepts that initial key handoff, your encryption is suddenly worthless. That's the classic weakness of symmetric encryption.
Asymmetric encryption gets clever. Instead of one shared key, you've got a pair — a

You hand out your Public Key freely. The Private Key, you keep to yourself. When someone wants to send you something encrypted, they use your Public Key to encrypt it. Then you use your Private Key to decrypt. That's asymmetric encryption.

Notice what's happening — the key pair is generated locally on the recipient's machine. The Public Key gets sent to the sender, who uses it to encrypt a message and sends it back. The recipient decrypts with their Private Key. Since the Private Key never leaves the local machine, even if someone intercepts the Public Key in transit, it doesn't matter — they can't use it to decrypt.

That's the basic idea behind symmetric vs. asymmetric encryption. In practice we use specific algorithms — AES, RSA, etc. — but the underlying mechanic is always "do encryption/decryption with a key".

GitHub's SSH is asymmetric. When you add an SSH key to GitHub, you're handing over your Public Key. GitHub encrypts with your Public Key during the handshake, and your machine decrypts with the matching Private Key.
Each contains specific algorithms (AES, RSA, etc.) — all variations on "do crypto with a key".
So when you use encryption, you have to be careful with key management — otherwise you've got security problems. Even with asymmetric, if your Private Key leaks, it's game over.
"Wait — JWT, the thing frontend devs use to talk to the backend, which kind of encryption is that?"
Honestly, JWT operationally uses keys and algorithms, but strictly speaking JWT isn't encryption — it's signing.

Why do I say that? JWT's job is verifying data integrity during transmission, not hiding the data. So you usually receive a JWT on the client without trying to decrypt it; you just pass it along. You don't even have the key. JWT verifies that the data hasn't been tampered with — the backend handles verification, and the frontend just uses the token (since the frontend can't keep secrets anyway).
After all that crypto talk, time to cover something often confused with encryption —
Why Base64? Because it's the easiest to confuse for encryption. Burn this in:
Base64 is not encryption — it's encoding.
Why? Because Base64 follows a known, reversible rule. Anyone who knows it's Base64 can decode it back to the original.

Base64 works in binary, so we convert each value to binary:
Each ASCII value is padded to 8 bits.
Base64 takes 6-bit chunks, so we re-slice those 8-bit binary values into 6-bit groups:
How do we verify? Use an online tool like
Same result. And we can decode "

So Base64 is fully reversible. That means it's not suitable for protecting sensitive data — emails, passwords, ID numbers — because anyone who knows it's Base64 can decode it. Base64 is fine for simple encoding tasks like embedding an image as a Base64 data URI in HTML (saves an extra HTTP request, at the cost of bigger HTML).
That's pretty much it for this one — hope it helped.

Something came up recently that made me want to write a beginner-level post on encryption. This one covers what encryption actually is, why we use it, the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption (with examples like AES, RSA, and how GitHub SSH works), and a section on Base64 — which a lot of people mistake for encryption but is actually just encoding.
